Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 1 (AUTOMATE → node) + Rule 4 (group tightly coupled steps)
Consolidation Rationale: All four data retrieval steps (1-4) share the same logic pattern: API call with account_id + quarter date range. Different data sources but identical node behavior — call API, store result in state. Grouping into one node keeps the graph topology simple while internal parallelism handles concurrency.
Notes: The four API calls within this node are independent and can be parallelised internally using async calls or Send()

Consolidation Rationale: Same logic pattern as Step 1: API call with account_id + date range. The tool differs (Support API vs CRM API) but the node behavior is identical — retrieve data and store in state. No reason to create a separate node for each data source when the pattern is the same.
Notes: If the support API returns paginated results the node handles pagination internally

Consolidation Rationale: Same logic pattern as Steps 1-2: API call with account_id + date range. Usage data API wrapper returns a single dict of metrics. Grouping with other retrieval steps avoids a graph with four sequential data-fetch nodes that could be one.
Notes: Usage data includes DAU/MAU and feature adoption metrics

Consolidation Rationale: Same logic pattern as Steps 1-3: API call with account_id + date range. Slack search returns a list of thread dicts. Completes the data retrieval group — all four sources are fetched by one node with four internal tool calls.
Notes: Slack threads use Annotated[list[dict] with operator.add] reducer in state to support potential future fan-out

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 2 (HIL candidate) → Consolidation Principle 1 (merge related reviews)
Consolidation Rationale: Steps 5-7 each have HIL elements (sentiment validation / ticket interpretation / usage context) but the human reviews related dimensions of the same account. Presenting all three analyses together in one interrupt is faster and produces better feedback — the reviewer can cross-reference findings across dimensions. The sentiment review merges into the analysis checkpoint rather than being its own interrupt.
Notes: The HIL review for this step is deferred to the hil_review_analysis node which surfaces all three analyses together

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 1 (AUTOMATE component) + Consolidation Principle 1 (HIL component merged into single checkpoint)
Consolidation Rationale: Split boundary step: the quantitative analysis (calculate volume trends / severity distribution / MTTR vs SLA / CSAT) is automated as processing logic inside analyse_health. The HIL component (interpreting edge cases and validating scores) is consolidated with Steps 5 and 7 into the hil_review_analysis checkpoint. Splitting follows the methodology's instruction to decompose split-boundary steps before applying rules.
Notes: SLA thresholds and scoring criteria come from an external scoring rubric config file — not hardcoded in the node

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 1 (AUTOMATE component) + Consolidation Principle 1 (HIL component merged into single checkpoint)
Consolidation Rationale: Same pattern as Step 6: quantitative analysis (DAU/MAU trends / feature adoption rates / API error rates) is automated inside analyse_health. The HIL review component is consolidated with Steps 5 and 6 into one checkpoint. The coupling criterion is that all three outputs are reviewed as a unit — they represent different dimensions of the same account's health.
Notes: Adoption benchmarks come from the scoring rubric config. analyse_health runs three separate LLM prompts (one per dimension)

Consolidation Rule Applied: Consolidation Principle 1 (merge related reviews into single checkpoint)
Consolidation Rationale: This node is the consolidated HIL checkpoint for Steps 5-7. Rather than three separate interrupts (one per analysis dimension) the graph pauses once and surfaces all three analyses together. The reviewer gets full context to cross-reference findings and can correct any dimension in a single interaction. This eliminates context-switching overhead and produces higher-quality feedback.
Notes: Uses Feedback-as-Context pattern: human provides freeform text stored in analysis_human_feedback. Unconditional edge to synthesise.

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 2 (HIL candidate) → Consolidation Principle 2 (upstream checkpoint converts downstream HIL to automated)
Consolidation Rationale: Originally tagged HIL because risk identification requires judgement. But after hil_review_analysis the human has already validated (and potentially corrected) every analysis input that feeds this step. With confirmed inputs and a well-defined scoring framework the synthesis becomes deterministic enough to automate. The second checkpoint (hil_review_report) still catches any synthesis errors in the final output.
Notes: Human corrections from hil_review_analysis flow forward via analysis_human_feedback in state so synthesise operates on vetted data

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 2 (HIL candidate) → Consolidation Principle 2 (upstream checkpoint converts downstream HIL to automated)
Consolidation Rationale: Same reasoning as Step 8: the health score is derived from analysis outputs the human has already confirmed. The scoring framework (Green/Amber/Red with defined thresholds) is deterministic given validated inputs. An additional interrupt here would add overhead without meaningfully improving accuracy since the report review checkpoint catches scoring errors.
Notes: Health scoring thresholds come from the health_scoring section of the scoring rubric config

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 1 (AUTOMATE component) + Rule 4 (group with Step 11) + Consolidation Principle 1 (HIL component → hil_review_report)
Consolidation Rationale: Split boundary step: report drafting is automated (LLM fills a structured template with sections for each health dimension). The HIL review component is consolidated with Step 11's review into a single hil_review_report checkpoint. Steps 10 and 11 are grouped into one node because they share the same tool (LLM) and produce a single deliverable package (report + summary).
Notes: Report uses a seven-section template: Account Overview / Ticket Health / Product Engagement / Sentiment / Risks & Opportunities / Health Score / Recommended Actions

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 1 (AUTOMATE component) + Rule 4 (group with Step 10) + Consolidation Principle 1 (HIL component → hil_review_report)
Consolidation Rationale: Same pattern as Step 10: exec summary generation is automated (LLM produces a 3-5 sentence summary from the completed report). The HIL review is consolidated into hil_review_report. Grouping with Step 10 is natural — the exec summary is derived from the report so they share inputs and are produced in sequence within the same node.
Notes: The exec summary prompt receives the completed report_markdown as input — it is generated after the full report

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 2 (HIL → interrupt node) + Consolidation Principle 1 (merge related reviews)
Consolidation Rationale: This node is the consolidated HIL checkpoint for the report package (Steps 10-11). The human reviews the complete deliverable — full report and exec summary — in a single interaction. Uses Feedback-as-Control-Flow pattern because the human can approve / revise (report only) / rework (re-run analysis). Each decision routes to a different node.
Notes: Conditional edges: approved → END; revise → generate_report; rework → analyse_health. Decision stored in report_review_decision.

Consolidation Rule Applied: Rule 3 (MANUAL steps are outside the graph)
Consolidation Rationale: MANUAL steps happen after the graph completes. The graph ends when hil_review_report approves the output. Final edits / distribution list decisions / the actual send all happen outside graph.invoke(). The graph boundary ends at the last point where the agent adds value.
Notes: Step 12 was tagged MANUAL in Stage 3 because distribution involves relationship decisions and channel choices that require human judgement
